metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Literary Studies 10 focuses on the analysis of a wide range of literary texts — fiction, poetry, drama, and non-fiction — to build students' critical and creative thinking. Students examine how texts are socially, culturally, and historically constructed, how language shapes identity, and how First Peoples narrative traditions contribute to Canadian literary culture. Students read for enjoyment and personal growth as well as for critical understanding.
metadata.general_description: A focused study of literary texts across genres, developing skills in critical analysis, personal response, and understanding of how literature constructs meaning within its social and cultural contexts.
metadata.grade_10_module_note: Grade 10 ELA is modular. Students typically take Literary Studies 10 as part of their ELA requirement, often paired with Composition 10 or another module.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Literature gives us access to perspectives, experiences, and ideas we might never encounter directly — and deepens our understanding of what it means to be human.

big_ideas.statement: People understand text differently depending on their world views and perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Readers bring their own identities and worldviews to texts, and this shapes interpretation — which is why diverse readers can have legitimately different responses to the same work.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every text is a product of its context: who wrote it, when, where, and for whom all shape what it says and how.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: The words we choose are never neutral — they carry power, assumptions, cultural weight, and the capacity to change how people think and feel.

big_ideas.statement: Questioning what we hear, read, and view contributes to our ability to be educated and engaged citizens.
big_ideas.elaboration: Critical reading and media literacy are civic skills — they help us navigate information, resist manipulation, and participate thoughtfully in democratic life.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Read for enjoyment and personal goals.
i_can_statements: I can read texts I choose myself for pleasure and personal growth.
I can set reading goals and reflect on my progress toward them.
I can discuss what I enjoy about reading and why certain texts appeal to me.

competencies.raw: Recognize and appreciate the role of story, narrative, and oral tradition in expressing First Peoples perspectives.
i_can_statements: I can explain how oral tradition functions as law, history, and cultural memory in First Peoples communities.
I can identify the values and knowledge embedded in a First Peoples story.
I can engage with First Peoples texts using cultural protocols and respectful awareness.

competencies.raw: Recognize diversity within First Peoples societies represented in texts.
i_can_statements: I can identify the specific community or nation represented in a First Peoples text.
I can explain why treating all First Peoples as a single group is inaccurate and harmful.
I can compare texts from different First Peoples communities and discuss what makes each unique.

competencies.raw: Recognize the influence of place in First Peoples and other Canadian texts.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how a specific place shapes the identity and relationships in a text.
I can explain how land and territory function in First Peoples texts as more than setting.
I can connect a Canadian text's sense of place to its cultural or historical context.

competencies.raw: Access information for diverse purposes; evaluate relevance, accuracy, and reliability.
i_can_statements: I can find relevant background information to deepen my understanding of a literary text.
I can evaluate the credibility of secondary sources about a text or author.
I can use context clues, biographical details, and historical information to enrich my analysis.

competencies.raw: Apply comprehension strategies to multimodal texts; guide inquiry and extend thinking.
i_can_statements: I can apply reading strategies (predict, infer, question, summarize, visualize) to complex literary texts.
I can extend my thinking about a text by raising new questions it prompts.
I can apply comprehension strategies to visual texts, film, and digital media.

competencies.raw: Think critically about ideas within and beyond texts; identify bias, contradictions, and distortions.
i_can_statements: I can analyze a text's central ideas and evaluate how the author develops them.
I can identify bias or distortion in a text and explain its effect.
I can consider whose perspectives are represented and whose are missing in a text.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Exchange ideas respectfully from diverse perspectives to build shared understanding.
i_can_statements: I can engage in literary discussion, sharing my interpretation and listening carefully to others.
I can respectfully disagree with a classmate's interpretation and explain my reasoning.
I can build shared meaning through collaborative literary conversation.

competencies.raw: Respond to text in personal, creative, and critical ways.
i_can_statements: I can write a personal response to a text that is grounded in the text but also my own experience.
I can create a creative response — in a new form or genre — that demonstrates genuine engagement with the original.
I can write a critical analysis of a literary text using specific textual evidence.

competencies.raw: Refine texts to improve clarity and impact; express opinions with evidence.
i_can_statements: I can revise my literary analysis to improve the clarity of my argument.
I can support my literary interpretations with specific, well-chosen quotations and evidence.
I can state and defend a clear position on a literary question.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate speaking and listening skills in formal and informal contexts.
i_can_statements: I can participate confidently in a literary seminar or Socratic dialogue.
I can listen actively and respond thoughtfully to others' literary interpretations.
I can present a literary analysis clearly to an audience.

competencies.raw: Use writing and design processes for multiple purposes and audiences; use proper citations.
i_can_statements: I can write literary analysis essays using a clear structure and academic conventions.
I can properly cite quotations and sources in my literary writing.
I can adapt my writing style to different purposes (personal response vs. analytical essay).

areas.name: Text Forms, Features, and Genres
areas.raw: Literary or thematic categories including adventure, fable, fairy tale, fantasy, folklore, historical, horror, legend, mystery, mythology, biography, essay, journalism, memoir, personal narrative, and speech.
topics: Fiction genres: adventure, fable, fairy tale, fantasy, folklore, historical fiction, horror, legend, mystery, mythology, science fiction
Non-fiction forms: biography, memoir, essay, journalism, personal narrative, speech
Drama: stage, screenplay, and radio play
Poetry: lyric, narrative, dramatic, concrete, spoken word
Protocols related to ownership of First Peoples oral texts

areas.name: Text Features and Structures
areas.raw: Typography, font style, titles, diagrams, captions, maps, charts, illustrations, photographs; narrative structures in First Peoples texts (circular, iterative, cyclical).
topics: Visual text elements: typography, layout, illustration, photography
Narrative structures: linear, cyclical, iterative
First Peoples narrative structures: circular, iterative, cyclical
Non-linear structures: frame narrative, in medias res, multiple perspectives

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies, oral language strategies, metacognitive strategies, writing processes.
topics: Literary reading strategies: predicting, inferring, questioning, visualizing, identifying theme, summarizing
Note-taking and annotation for literary analysis
Metacognitive reflection on reading experience
Writing process for literary analysis: prewriting, drafting, revising, editing

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Elements of style (diction, vocabulary, sentence structure, tone); voice (point of view, humour, irony, satire); literary elements and devices; citation techniques.
topics: Elements of style: diction, sentence structure, tone, mood
Voice and point of view: first person, second person, third person limited/omniscient
Irony: dramatic, situational, verbal
Satire and humour as literary modes
Literary devices: metaphor, simile, symbolism, allegory, foreshadowing, flashback, allusion
Theme identification and analysis
Citation techniques: MLA format for literary analysis
